name: What to Do When Your Injury Prevents You from Returning to Your Shift
description: What to Do When Your Injury Prevents You from Returning to Your Shift - Salt Lake City

step.name: Step 1
step.text: Finding out that your workplace injury is severe enough to prevent you from returning to your previous job is a life-altering moment. Whether you are in Salt Lake City, Boise, or the surrounding areas, the transition from being an active worker to dealing with a long-term disability is fraught with legal and financial hurdles. If your doctor has officially stated that you cannot return to your regular duties, you must act decisively to protect your future.

step.name: Step 2
step.text: ### What to Do First

step.name: Step 3
step.text: The moment it becomes clear that you cannot return to work, your priority must shift from short-term recovery to long-term stability.

step.name: Step 4
step.text: 1. **Request the "Work Status Report":** Ensure your treating physician provides a written report clearly detailing your permanent restrictions. Generic statements like "cannot lift heavy objects" are insufficient; you need specifics (e.g., "cannot lift more than 10 pounds," "no repeated bending").

step.name: Step 5
step.text: 2. **Communicate with Your Employer:** Provide a copy of these restrictions to your supervisor or HR department immediately. Do this via email or certified mail so there is a paper trail.

step.name: Step 6
step.text: 3. **Inquire About Accommodations:** Explicitly ask if the company has "light duty" or alternative positions that fit within your medical restrictions.

step.name: Step 7
step.text: 4. **Document the Response:** If they tell you there is no work available, get that in writing. This "Lack of Work" documentation is critical for your workers' compensation claim.

step.name: Step 8
step.text: ### How the Process Works

step.name: Step 9
step.text: In Utah and Idaho, the workers' compensation system is designed to provide "Temporary Total Disability" (TTD) benefits while you are healing. However, once you reach "Maximum Medical Improvement" (MMI)—the point where your condition is unlikely to improve further—the system shifts.

step.name: Step 10
step.text: If you still cannot return to work at MMI, the insurance carrier will likely attempt to stop your weekly checks. This is the "Gap Period" where many workers fall through the cracks. At this stage, you may be eligible for:

step.name: Step 11
step.text: - **Permanent Partial Disability (PPD):** Compensation for the permanent loss of function of a body part.

step.name: Step 12
step.text: - **Permanent Total Disability (PTD):** If your injuries are so severe that you can no longer perform any gainful employment in the labor market.

step.name: Step 13
step.text: - **Vocational Rehabilitation:** Programs designed to retrain you for a different career path that fits your physical limitations.

step.name: Step 14
step.text: ### Key Evidence and Documents

step.name: Step 15
step.text: To prove you are unable to return to work, you need more than just a doctor's note. You need a comprehensive "Case File" including:

step.name: Step 16
step.text: - **Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE):** A standardized test that measures your physical capabilities (lifting, pulling, standing) over several hours.

step.name: Step 17
step.text: - **Vocational Assessment:** An expert report analyzing your education, work history, and physical limits to determine if any jobs actually exist for you in the Salt Lake City or Boise markets.

step.name: Step 18
step.text: - **Personnel Records:** To prove what your job duties actually were before the injury, ensuring the insurance company doesn't downplay the physical requirements of your role.

step.name: Step 19
step.text: ### Common Mistakes to Avoid

step.name: Step 20
step.text: - **Resigning Voluntarily:** Never quit your job without consulting an attorney. If you resign, the insurance company may argue you "voluntarily withdrew" from the workforce, which can disqualify you from certain benefits.

step.name: Step 21
step.text: - **Exaggerating or Understating Limits:** During an FCE, be honest. If you try to look "tougher" than you are, you end up with restrictions that allow you to be sent back to a job you can't actually do. If you "fake" a limitation, the evaluator will catch it, and your credibility will be destroyed.

step.name: Step 22
step.text: ### When to Contact a Lawyer

step.name: Step 23
step.text: The stakes are highest when you cannot return to work. You are no longer just fighting for a medical bill to be paid; you are fighting for your ability to provide for your family for the rest of your life. You should contact Just Work Comp Law if:

step.name: Step 24
step.text: - Your employer refuses to accommodate your restrictions but the insurance company wants to stop your checks.

step.name: Step 25
step.text: - You have been offered a "settlement" that seems low given your inability to work.

step.name: Step 26
step.text: - The insurance company’s doctor says you are "fine" while your own body tells you otherwise.

step.name: Step 27
step.text: ### Next Steps

step.name: Step 28
step.text: 1. Gather your most recent medical records and any correspondence from your employer regarding your job status.

step.name: Step 29
step.text: 2. Review your "Job Description" and note every task you can no longer perform.

step.name: Step 30
step.text: 3. Call 801-746-0290 for a free consultation. Our attorneys, including Halston T. Davis and Mark J. Sanchez, specialize in these complex "unable to return" cases in Utah and Idaho.
